module.title: TABLES AS MAPS
module.subtitle: pairs · key/value · membership · frequency
module.theory:
## Tables can be maps, not just lists
In the last sector a table was a numbered list (`t[1]`, `t[2]`, ...). The SAME table type can also be a **map** (also called a hash, dictionary, or lookup): you store a **value** under a **key**, and the key can be a string.

~~~lua
local user = {}
user["name"] = "lain"   -- store value "lain" under key "name"
user["level"] = 7
print(user["name"])      -- lain
~~~

**WHAT** — `t[key] = value` puts something into the slot named `key`. **WHY** — a map lets you look things up *by name* instead of by position, which is how you model records, settings, and counts.

> INTEL — `t.name` is just shorthand for `t["name"]`. The dot form only works when the key is a plain word; for any other key (a variable, or a string with spaces) you must use the square brackets `t[key]`.

## Reading a key — and the nil that means "absent"
Reading `t[key]` gives you the stored value. If nothing was ever stored under that key, you get **nil** (Lua's word for "nothing here"). It is NOT an error and it does NOT crash.

~~~lua
local scores = {}
scores["ana"] = 50
print(scores["ana"])   -- 50
print(scores["zed"])   -- nil   (never stored, so absent)
~~~

> WARNING — a missing key returns `nil`, and `nil` can't do arithmetic. `scores["zed"] + 1` blows up with "attempt to perform arithmetic on a nil value". Always check, or supply a default (see below).

## A safe default with `or`
A common trick: `t[key] or default` gives the stored value, or the default when the slot is `nil`. This is how you start a counter at 0 even on the first sighting.

~~~lua
local n = counts["hit"] or 0   -- 0 if "hit" was never seen
counts["hit"] = n + 1          -- now it's safely a number
~~~

## Iterating a map with `pairs`
A numeric `for` only walks 1, 2, 3, ... so it can't reach string keys. To visit EVERY key/value in a map, use **pairs**:

~~~lua
for key, value in pairs(scores) do
  print(key, value)   -- ana  50   (and every other pair)
end
~~~

**WHAT** — `pairs(t)` hands you each `key, value` pair in turn. **WHY** — it's the only way to loop over a map, since the keys aren't numbered positions.

> WARNING — `pairs` gives NO guaranteed order. Do not rely on which key comes first. When you need a total or a count, accumulate into a variable inside the loop — never assume the pairs arrive sorted.

## Checking membership
"Is this key in the map?" — compare its value to `nil`. If the slot holds anything, the key is present.

~~~lua
if scores["ana"] ~= nil then
  print("ana is registered")
end
~~~

Note `~=` is Lua's **not-equal** operator (other languages write `!=`). And `t[k] ~= nil` is the precise membership test: it stays true even if the stored value is `0` or `""`, which a plain `if t[k] then` would mistreat — but in Lua only `nil` and `false` are falsy, so `0` and `""` are still truthy. Using `~= nil` makes the intent obvious.

## Building a frequency / count table
Put it together: walk a list, and for each item bump its counter in a map. This counts how often each value appears.

~~~lua
local items = { "a", "b", "a" }
local freq = {}
for i = 1, #items do
  local k = items[i]
  freq[k] = (freq[k] or 0) + 1   -- start at 0, then add 1
end
print(freq["a"])   -- 2
print(freq["b"])   -- 1
~~~

> INTEL — `(freq[k] or 0) + 1` is the heartbeat of every counter you'll ever write. The `or 0` covers the first time you meet a key; after that the real number takes over.

> WARNING — remember Lua basics from earlier sectors: there is no `++`, so write `x = x + 1`. And `..` joins strings while `+` adds numbers — never use `+` to glue text or `..` to add.

exercises.title: STASH SLOT
exercises.brief: Store one value under a key.
exercises.prompt:
Define **stash(key, value)** that creates a fresh table, stores **value** under **key**, and returns the table.
~~~lua
local t = stash("name", "lain")
print(t["name"])  -- lain
~~~
exercises.hint: Inside the table, write t[key] = value before returning t.
exercises.lore: Every secret needs a slot in the grid. Name it, drop it, walk away.

exercises.title: COLD LOOKUP
exercises.brief: Read a key that might be missing.
exercises.prompt:
Define **lookup(t, key)** that returns the value stored under **key** in table **t**, or returns **nil** when the key is absent (just reading `t[key]` already does this).
~~~lua
local t = { name = "lain" }
print(lookup(t, "name"))  -- lain
print(lookup(t, "age"))   -- nil
~~~
exercises.starter:
function lookup(t, key)
  -- TODO: return the value at key (nil when absent)
  return ""
end
exercises.hint: Just return t[key] — Lua hands back nil for a key that was never set.
exercises.lore: Probe the slot. Empty slots whisper nil, not error.

exercises.title: REGISTERED
exercises.prompt:
Define **is_registered(t, key)** that returns **true** when **key** exists in **t**, and **false** when it does not. Test membership with `t[key] ~= nil`.
~~~lua
local users = { ana = true, zed = false }
print(is_registered(users, "ana"))  -- true
print(is_registered(users, "who"))  -- false
~~~
exercises.starter:
function is_registered(t, key)
  -- TODO: true when key exists, false otherwise
  return false
end

tests.name: value false still counts as present

exercises.hint: Return the comparison itself: t[key] ~= nil already yields true or false.
exercises.lore: The gate doesn't care what you are — only that your slot isn't empty.

exercises.title: COUNTER BUMP
exercises.brief: Increment a counter that may not exist yet.
exercises.prompt:
Define **bump(t, key)** that adds 1 to the counter at **key** in table **t** (starting from 0 if it was absent), then returns the table. Use the `(t[key] or 0) + 1` idiom.
~~~lua
local c = {}
bump(c, "hit")
bump(c, "hit")
print(c["hit"])  -- 2
~~~
exercises.starter:
function bump(t, key)
  -- TODO: add 1 to t[key], starting from 0 if absent
  return t
end
exercises.solution:
function bump(t, key)
  t[key] = (t[key] or 0) + 1
  return t
end

tests.name: keys are independent
tests.code:
local c = {}
bump(c, "a")
bump(c, "b")
bump(c, "a")
assert(c["a"] == 2 and c["b"] == 1)

exercises.hint: t[key] = (t[key] or 0) + 1 — the 'or 0' covers the first sighting.
exercises.lore: Tick the ledger. Each ghost in the wire leaves one more mark.

exercises.title: KEY CENSUS
exercises.brief: Count how many keys a map holds.
exercises.prompt:
Define **key_count(t)** that returns how many key/value pairs are in map **t**. The `#` operator does NOT work for string keys — loop with **pairs** and count.
~~~lua
key_count({ a = 1, b = 2, c = 3 })  -- 3
key_count({})                        -- 0
~~~
exercises.starter:
function key_count(t)
  local n = 0
  -- TODO: walk t with pairs and count each key
  return -1
end
exercises.solution:
function key_count(t)
  local n = 0
  for _ in pairs(t) do
    n = n + 1
  end
  return n
end
exercises.hint: for _ in pairs(t) do n = n + 1 end — then return n.
exercises.lore: Tally the slots. The grid never tells you its size — you count it yourself.

exercises.title: FREQUENCY TALLY
exercises.brief: Build a count table from a list.
exercises.prompt:
Define **tally(list)** that takes an array of strings and returns a map from each string to how many times it appears. Walk the array with a numeric for loop and bump a counter for each item.
~~~lua
local f = tally({ "a", "b", "a" })
print(f["a"])  -- 2
print(f["b"])  -- 1
~~~
exercises.solution:
function tally(list)
  local freq = {}
  for i = 1, #list do
    local k = list[i]
    freq[k] = (freq[k] or 0) + 1
  end
  return freq
end

tests.name: counts repeats
tests.code:
local f = tally({ "a", "b", "a" })
assert(f["a"] == 2 and f["b"] == 1)

tests.name: single occurrences
tests.code:
local f = tally({ "x", "y", "z" })
assert(f["x"] == 1 and f["y"] == 1 and f["z"] == 1)

tests.name: unseen item is nil
tests.code:
local f = tally({ "a", "a", "a" })
assert(f["a"] == 3 and f["b"] == nil)

exercises.hint: Loop i = 1, #list; let k = list[i]; freq[k] = (freq[k] or 0) + 1.
exercises.lore: Feed it the packet stream. It spits back who screamed loudest.

exercises.title: LOUDEST SIGNAL
exercises.brief: Find the key with the largest value.
exercises.prompt:
Define **top_key(counts)** that takes a map from string to number and returns the key whose value is the largest. Walk the map with **pairs**, tracking the best key and its value. Assume the map is non-empty and the maximum is unique.
~~~lua
top_key({ a = 1, b = 5, c = 2 })  -- "b"
~~~
exercises.starter:
function top_key(counts)
  -- TODO: return the key with the biggest value
  return nil
end
exercises.solution:
function top_key(counts)
  local best_key = nil
  local best_val = nil
  for k, v in pairs(counts) do
    if best_val == nil or v > best_val then
      best_key = k
      best_val = v
    end
  end
  return best_key
end

tests.name: works with built count map

exercises.hint: Seed best_val = nil; for k, v in pairs(counts) do if best_val == nil or v > best_val then ... end end.
exercises.lore: Somewhere in the static, one voice peaks. Lock onto it.

exercises.title: MERGE LEDGERS
exercises.prompt:
Define **merge_counts(a, b)** that returns a NEW map where each key's value is the sum of its values in **a** and **b** (a missing key counts as 0). Copy **a** with pairs, then add **b** with pairs.
~~~lua
local m = merge_counts({ x = 1, y = 2 }, { y = 3, z = 5 })
print(m["x"])  -- 1
print(m["y"])  -- 5
print(m["z"])  -- 5
~~~
exercises.solution:
function merge_counts(a, b)
  local out = {}
  for k, v in pairs(a) do
    out[k] = (out[k] or 0) + v
  end
  for k, v in pairs(b) do
    out[k] = (out[k] or 0) + v
  end
  return out
end

tests.name: overlapping key sums
tests.code:
local m = merge_counts({ x = 1, y = 2 }, { y = 3, z = 5 })
assert(m["x"] == 1 and m["y"] == 5 and m["z"] == 5)

tests.name: no overlap keeps both
tests.code:
local m = merge_counts({ a = 4 }, { b = 6 })
assert(m["a"] == 4 and m["b"] == 6)

tests.name: result is a fresh table
tests.code:
local a = { p = 1 }
local m = merge_counts(a, {})
m["p"] = 99
assert(a["p"] == 1)

exercises.hint: Two pairs loops, each doing out[k] = (out[k] or 0) + v.
exercises.lore: Two ledgers, one truth. Sum the wire and seal it.
